preamble: The transformation from a voiceless dental fricative (θ) to a voiceless aspirated alveolar plosive (tʰ) is a phonetic shift characterized by fortition. This often occurs when a language lacks the dental fricative in its native phonological inventory and substitutes it with a phonetically similar, but more 'fortis' (stronger) sound, such as an aspirated stop, particularly in the context of loanword adaptation.
phoneticEffects: Fortition (fricative to plosive), change of manner (fricative to plosive), change of place (dental to alveolar), and addition of aspiration

examples.note: Adaptation of the English word 'authority' into Urdu, where /θ/ is substituted with /tʰ/.

sources: Hussain, S., & Khan, S. (2024). Phonological Adaptation of English Loan Words in Urdu: A Natural Phonology Approach. Pakistan Languages and Humanities Review, 4(2), 1-10.
